title: Game Theory: Strategic Thinking
subtitle: Nash equilibrium, prisoner's dilemma, auctions, negotiation, and repeated games
description: Game theory is not about games. It is a mathematical and behavioral tool for decision-making in situations where your outcome depends on what others do. This checklist covers key concepts: types of games (zero-sum and positive-sum), Nash equilibrium, the prisoner's dilemma, repeated games and tit-for-tat strategy, auctions and the winner's curse, Stackelberg games, games against nature, and expected utility theory. You'll learn to analyze negotiations, market competition, everyday conflicts, and even influence the rules of the game to your advantage.

items.title: Prisoner's Dilemma: How Individual Interest Kills the Common Good
items.source: Albert W. Tucker (1950), formalization; Robert Axelrod — The Evolution of Cooperation (1984)
items.text: Two prisoners are interrogated in separate rooms. Each has a choice: stay silent (cooperate) or confess (defect). If both stay silent — 1 year each. If both confess — 5 years each. If one confesses and the other stays silent — the confessor goes free immediately, the silent one gets 10 years. From an individual perspective, confessing is always more profitable (dominant strategy). But the result is both confess and get 5 years each, when they could have gotten 1 year each by staying silent. This is the dilemma: individual rationality leads to collective disaster. Prisoner's dilemmas are everywhere: price wars (we lower prices — we both lose, but each fears the other will lower), arms races, traffic jams (each thinks their 'one time' won't matter), environmental pollution. Key solution: if the game is repeated many times, cooperation becomes possible through punishment and reputation mechanisms.
items.tip: When you see a situation where everyone acts against the common good, ask: is this a one-shot or repeated game? If repeated, suggest a simple rule: 'I cooperate as long as you cooperate; you defect once — I defect on the next move' (tit-for-tat strategy). This triggers cooperation.

items.title: Nash Equilibrium: The Point Where No One Wants to Change Their Decision
items.text: A Nash equilibrium is a set of strategies where no player can improve their outcome by unilaterally changing their strategy, assuming others keep theirs unchanged. This is not necessarily the 'best' or 'fairest' outcome. It is a stable prediction: if everyone plays a Nash equilibrium, no one has an incentive to deviate. Example: in the prisoner's dilemma, the Nash equilibrium is both confess (even though it's worse for both than both staying silent). Example 2: choosing a driving side — two Nash equilibria (everyone drives on the right or everyone on the left), but it doesn't matter which, as long as everyone chooses the same. Most games have multiple Nash equilibria. The analyst's task is to predict which one will be realized (cultural norms, focal points, history of interactions). Nash equilibrium is a standard prediction tool in economics, political science, and biology (evolutionarily stable strategies).
items.tip: When making a decision, ask: 'If I do X now, will the other player have an incentive to do Y? And if I do Z — will their best response change?' Find a set of actions where neither you nor the other wants to move first. That's a Nash equilibrium.

items.title: Repeated Games and the Tit-for-Tat Strategy
items.source: Robert Axelrod — The Evolution of Cooperation (1984), computer strategy tournament
items.text: In a one-shot prisoner's dilemma, defection is the dominant strategy. But if the same game is repeated many times with the same players, cooperation becomes possible. Robert Axelrod ran a famous tournament of computer strategies for the repeated prisoner's dilemma. The simplest strategy won: 'Tit-for-Tat'. Its algorithm: first move — cooperate. Each subsequent move — simply repeat the opponent's last move. Properties of Tit-for-Tat: nice (never defects first), retaliatory (immediately punishes defection), forgiving (returns to cooperation if the opponent returns), simple (easily recognized by others). This creates long-term cooperation without central control. Conversely, if the game has no known end ('infinite horizon'), cooperation is stable. If the end is known ('last move'), both will defect on the last move — and this unravels defection backwards (the end-game paradox).
items.tip: In long-term relationships (with a colleague, supplier, neighbor), consciously play Tit-for-Tat. Start with a cooperative action. Respond to defection with symmetrical defection (once). When cooperation returns — forgive and return to cooperation. This disciplines the other side and doesn't turn you into a victim.

items.title: Zero-Sum and Positive-Sum Games
items.source: John von Neumann, Oskar Morgenstern — Theory of Games and Economic Behavior (1944)
items.text: A basic division of games by sum of payoffs. Zero-sum games: one player's gain equals another's loss. The total 'pie' is fixed. Examples: chess, tennis, most gambling, a lawsuit over a fixed amount. Here, the optimal strategy is to maximize your gain at the other's expense (minimax principle). Positive-sum games: the total gain can be greater than the sum of inputs through cooperation and value creation. Examples: trade (both sides benefit from exchange), joint ventures, negotiations with a union about increasing productivity, environmental agreements. Many people's mistake is perceiving any game as zero-sum ('if he wins, I lose'), missing win-win opportunities. True mastery is transforming the game: enlarge the pie before dividing it. Often, you first need to play a positive-sum game (create value), then a zero-sum game (divide it).
items.tip: Before negotiations, ask yourself: 'Is this a fixed pie or can it be enlarged?' If possible — first find ways to increase the total outcome (additional concessions, resources, timelines). Only then move to dividing. If the other person stubbornly sees the game as zero-sum — show them you can play tough too, but first offer cooperation.

items.title: Auctions and the Winner's Curse
items.source: Richard Thaler — The Winner's Curse (1988); William Vickrey (1961), Nobel Prize
items.text: In auctions, the winner often overpays. The 'winner's curse' occurs when an item's value is uncertain and each participant makes their own estimate. The winner is the one who gave the highest estimate. But their estimate is likely inflated relative to true value. As a result, the winner either overpays or gets a losing asset. Classic example: oil lease auctions in the Gulf of Mexico in the 1970s — winners often did not recover costs. Different auction types affect strategy: English (open bidding with increases) — allows information gathering; Dutch (price goes down) — pressures speed; first-price sealed-bid (winner pays their bid) — leads to underbidding; second-price sealed-bid (Vickrey) — winner pays second bidder's price — theoretically incentivizes bidding true value. Conclusion: always determine your maximum price in advance (based on your valuation and risks) and never exceed it during bidding, regardless of pressure.
items.tip: Before participating in any auction (from buying an apartment to a tender), do three steps. 1) Determine the objective value of the lot to you. 2) Reduce it by 20% — a correction for the winner's curse. 3) Write this figure on paper and do not exceed it, whatever emotions arise. Remember: if you win the auction, you may be the only one who misjudged the value.

items.title: Stackelberg Games: First-Mover Advantage
items.source: Heinrich von Stackelberg (1934) — Marktform und Gleichgewicht
items.text: In sequential (dynamic) games, the order of moves matters. In the Stackelberg duopoly model, one player — the leader — chooses output quantity first. The second player — the follower — observes the leader's choice and makes their own decision. The leader gains an advantage: they can take a larger market share, knowing the follower will adjust. In the Cournot model (simultaneous choice), the equilibrium is symmetric; in Stackelberg, the leader's profit is higher than the follower's, and even higher than in the Cournot equilibrium. A broader principle: in sequential games, whoever can make a credible commitment first gains an advantage. Examples: a company that first builds a large plant (creating excess capacity as a threat), a government that first sets a regulation (changes the rules of the game), a negotiator who names a price first (anchoring effect). But it's important that the leader's move is irreversible or costly to reverse — otherwise it's not a commitment, just information.
items.tip: If you can make the first move that significantly changes other players' incentives, do it. But ensure your move is visible, understandable, and hard to undo (public commitment, contract, investment in specific assets). If you are second — study the leader's move and look for niches they haven't covered, or propose an alternative move that changes the game itself.

items.title: Mixed Strategies: Playing Randomly
items.text: In some games, there is no pure-strategy equilibrium (where a player always chooses one action). Example: the game 'Matching Pennies'. Player A wins if the results match, Player B wins if they differ. If A always plays Heads, B always plays Tails and wins. If B always plays Tails, A switches to Heads — and so on endlessly. Solution: mixed strategy — random choice with a certain probability (e.g., 50/50). In a mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium, each player chooses probabilities so that the other is indifferent between their pure strategies (expected payoff from any pure strategy is the same). Mixed strategies are important in sports (tennis serve: sometimes left, sometimes right), business (random quality checks so subordinates can't anticipate), military (stealth and surprise). Practical conclusion: if your opponent can predict your action, you are vulnerable. Add controlled randomness.
items.tip: If you're in a situation where your actions are easily predictable and the opponent exploits that, introduce an element of randomness. A simple method: flip a coin or use a random number generator to choose between 2-3 options. The main thing is that the probabilities are optimal (e.g., 40% / 60%, not 50/50).

items.title: Evolutionary Game Theory: Strategies That Survive
items.source: John Maynard Smith, George R. Price (1973) — Evolution of Animal Behaviour
items.text: Evolutionary game theory applies game logic to biology. Instead of 'rational choice' — selection: strategies that yield higher fitness spread in a population. The key concept is the evolutionarily stable strategy (ESS). This is a strategy that, once adopted by most, cannot be invaded by any mutant strategy. ESS is a refinement of Nash equilibrium for dynamic populations. Classic example: 'Hawks and Doves'. Hawk fights until serious injury; Dove displays but retreats. Neither pure strategy is an ESS. Equilibrium is a mixed strategy (part of the population are Hawks, part Doves) or a conditional strategy ('Dove first, if attacked — Hawk'). Evolutionary game theory explains cooperation in animals, altruism, honest threat signals (why they are honest), sexual selection (why peacock tails are a quality signal). In business and culture, new practices spread as 'memes' — and the winners are not the most optimal, but the most resistant to invasion by alternatives.
items.tip: When introducing a new practice in a team (e.g., remote work or new software), think: is this strategy evolutionarily stable? Will it be pushed back to the old practice if some employees refuse? Create an environment where the new strategy yields better results even if a few keep doing things the old way.

items.title: Games Against Nature and Expected Utility Theory
items.source: John von Neumann, Oskar Morgenstern (1944); Daniel Kahneman, Amos Tversky (1979) — Prospect Theory
items.text: Not all games involve a rational opponent. In games against nature, the 'second player' is uncertainty: weather, market, randomness. Here, decision theory under uncertainty applies. The main tool is maximizing expected utility. Calculate: probability of outcome × utility of outcome, sum, choose the option with the highest sum. But humans systematically violate this model (e.g., fear small-probability catastrophes and overestimate high-probability certainties). Kahneman and Tversky showed people evaluate gains and losses relative to a reference point, not absolutely, and dislike losses more than they like gains (loss aversion). Prospect theory describes real behavior. For practical decisions: if you can repeat the game many times — follow expected utility (law of large numbers will work). If the game is one-shot with high stakes — account for your psychological biases.
items.tip: Create a table: decision options, possible outcomes, their probabilities (even approximate), and utility to you (not in money, but in happiness, health, time). Multiply and compare sums. This protects against emotional biases. For critical one-shot decisions, ask: 'What will I feel if this outcome occurs?' Don't ignore fear, but don't let it dominate.

items.title: Behavioral Game Theory: People Are Not Rational
items.source: Colin Camerer (2003) — Behavioral Game Theory; Kahneman, Tversky, Thaler
items.text: Classic game theory assumes 'Homo economicus': infinitely rational, selfish, with stable preferences. Behavioral game theory studies how real people (boundedly rational, social, emotional) play games. Experimental data show: people punish unfairness even at their own expense (altruistic punishment). In the Ultimatum Game, the proposer divides a sum, the responder can accept or reject (then both get nothing). Classic theory predicts: proposer gives minimal (e.g., 1%), responder accepts (1% is better than 0). In reality: proposers give 30-50%, responders reject offers below 20% (they want to punish unfairness). People also over-trust in the Trust Game and reciprocate trust. Conclusion: consider fairness, reputation, emotions, bounded rationality (people don't calculate all moves to the end). In negotiations, don't try to 'bleed them dry' — it provokes revenge. Offer a fair split, and you'll gain more in the long run.
items.tip: In negotiations, especially with strangers, don't rely on predictions of 'pure-strategy Nash equilibrium'. Make a move most people would consider 'fair' (e.g., a 50/50 split in the Ultimatum Game). If you're offered a very unequal split — know you have the social right to refuse, even if it's 'not optimal'. This sends a signal and changes future behavior.

a: A situation where all players get maximum possible payoff
A set of strategies where no player can improve their outcome by unilaterally changing their strategy
A strategy where players always cooperate

quiz.q: What is the essence of the prisoner's dilemma?
a: Individual rationality leads to collective loss

quiz.q: Which strategy won Axelrod's tournament on the repeated prisoner's dilemma?
a: Tit-for-Tat (start cooperating, then copy opponent's last move)

quiz.q: What is the 'winner's curse' in auctions?
a: The winner always overpays due to overestimation
The winner receives a prize but cannot collect it
The auction fails due to low bids

quiz.q: In the Ultimatum Game, classic game theory predicts that the responder will...
a: Accept any positive amount, even a very small one
